Name: adaptive_kv_cache_optimization
Title: Adaptive Key-Value Cache Optimization for Efficient LLM Inference on GPUs
Short Hypothesis: By dynamically optimizing the layout and management of key-value caches during LLM inference, we can significantly reduce memory bandwidth usage and improve latency without compromising model accuracy.
Related Work: Existing research has focused on optimizing the computational aspects of large language models (LLMs) and fixed cache strategies. For instance, CENT proposes a GPU-free system for LLM inference but does not address the dynamic optimization of cache layouts. The work on multi-model ML inference serves to enhance resource utilization but lacks a focus on adaptive cache management for individual models. Our proposal distinctly emphasizes the dynamic adaptation of cache layouts based on real-time profiling, which has not been systematically explored in the context of LLMs, making our approach novel and impactful.
Abstract: Large Language Models (LLMs) are becoming increasingly complex and memory-intensive, leading to challenges in efficient inference on standard GPUs. A significant bottleneck arises from the key-value (KV) cache, which stores context information crucial for autoregressive token generation. We propose a novel approach that dynamically optimizes the layout and management of KV caches based on real-time profiling during inference. By monitoring cache access patterns and adjusting the cache structure accordingly, we aim to reduce memory bandwidth usage and improve overall latency. Our method involves a combination of adaptive cache resizing, eviction strategies based on usage frequency, and coalescing access patterns to minimize round-trips to memory. We will evaluate our approach against standard KV cache implementations, reporting metrics such as wall-clock latency, throughput, and memory bandwidth utilization. This work promises to bridge the gap between hardware capabilities and the demands of modern LLMs, offering practical solutions that can be implemented in existing GPU infrastructures.
Experiments: 1. Implement a baseline KV cache management system for LLM inference. 2. Develop an adaptive KV cache optimization algorithm that utilizes real-time profiling data to adjust the cache layout and eviction policy. 3. Conduct A/B testing on hardware to compare the performance of the adaptive system against the baseline. 4. Measure and report p50/p99 latency, tokens-per-second, and memory bandwidth utilization. 5. Analyze the impact of various cache configurations on inference speed and memory efficiency using a range of LLMs.
Risk Factors and Limitations: 1. The adaptation mechanism may introduce overhead that could negate latency gains. 2. Performance may vary significantly across different models and batch sizes. 3. The method is contingent on accurate profiling, and any inaccuracies could lead to suboptimal cache management. 4. The complexity of implementing dynamic cache management may pose challenges in practical deployment.
